text: The surgeon creates a single large flap across the surgical field, detaching the median umbilical ligaments in the process.
Two 12x16 Pro Group meshes are visible in the abdomen, along with the necessary sutures for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon begins the medial dissection for the bilateral inguinal hernia repair, focusing on the area typically addressed first even in unilateral cases.
The surgeon works down the midline, visually identifying the pubis and Cooper's ligament within the surgical field.
The surgeon transitions to lateral dissection after completing the previous steps, focusing on the lateral anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects in the preperitoneal space, leaving some fat on the abdominal wall while searching for the cord structures located deeper in the surgical field.
The surgeon reduces a small direct hernia, avoiding the inguinal canal due to the absence of an indirect sac.
The surgeon observes the peritoneal flap without a hernia sac and proceeds with parietalization, manipulating the tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from the fat tissue overlying the iliac vessels, maintaining a clear dissection plane without skeletonizing the vessels.

The surgeon performs a search for a femoral hernia, examining the nodal tissue that appears to have not protruded. Subsequently, the surgeon begins parietalizing the cord structures on the right side.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for a lipoma, identifying a reasonably sized lipoma that may account for the patient's symptoms.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the cord to reach the apex, ensuring that the cord and testicle are not inadvertently pulled out during the procedure.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the lipoma, ensuring it is positioned far enough back to sit beneath the mesh without lifting its inferior edge. The lipoma is fully mobilized without compromising blood supply, allowing adequate space for mesh placement.
The surgeon moves to the left side, sweeping the cord structures downward in anticipation of finding similar anatomical features as observed on the right side.

The surgeon examines the surgical field, noting the absence of an indirect sac, and proceeds to carefully dissect along the anatomical structures to reach a small left-sided direct lipoma or direct hernia.
The surgeon identifies the anatomical plane between the bladder and the fat over the iliac vessels, carefully dissecting to maintain separation between these structures.

The surgeon prioritizes space creation for the inferior edge of the mesh on the left side, continuing to develop the area so the mesh can fit snugly in the groove and adhere to the wall along the iliac vessels, while preserving the fat over the iliacs.
The surgeon searches for the left side of the lipoma, which is visibly present, and reduces it by carefully sweeping the cord structures off the lipoma to avoid pulling the cord or testicle.
The surgeon identifies a blood vessel emanating from within the canal on the left side, which is not reducible. The vessel is ligated to facilitate further reduction of the lipoma for mesh placement.
The surgeon fully reduces the lipoma, creating ample space for mesh placement. The final view shows the surgical field prepared for mesh insertion.
The surgeon places a twelve by sixteen mesh on the right side, ensuring it fits securely within the prepared space adjacent to the iliacs.
The surgeon places the left-sided mesh into the prepared space, ensuring it fits snugly along the iliac wall while maintaining the surrounding fat tissue.

The surgeon ensures the anterior edges of the mesh oppose the sidewall to prevent tissue from sliding underneath. The flap is closed starting from the middle, then moving to the left and right. A small rent near the VAS joining the cord structures on the right side is repaired with a 3-0 Vicryl suture.
The surgeon places the lipoma back into the flap, positioning it on the inner surface of the mesh within the surgical field.
The surgeon places the right lipoma back into the flap and proceeds to close the flap, ensuring proper alignment and closure.

The surgeon uses an angiocath inserted centrally into the flap, connected to suction, while applying compression with a grasper to remove gas and ensure the mesh remains flat without clamshelling or folding.
